title: Read UFW Policy Verbosely
hook: The firewall was active, but the defaults mattered more than the rule list.
problem: You need the UFW default policy, logging state, and allowed inbound rules in one readable snapshot.
starting_state: A VPS-style fixture has UFW enabled with several inbound allow and deny rules.
what_changed: Nothing changes. UFW prints the current policy and rule summary.
when_to_use: Use during exposure checks, handoffs, or after a deploy changes which services should be reachable.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume UFW is the only firewall layer on every host; nftables, iptables, cloud firewalls, and provider rules may also apply.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: UFW status, default policy, logging state, and inbound rules.

shorts.title: Read the firewall defaults.
shorts.voiceover: A firewall rule list is not enough. UFW verbose output shows whether incoming traffic is denied by default.
shorts.caption: Default policy explains the rules.

linkedin.question: Do you check firewall defaults before reading individual rules?
linkedin.cta: Start exposure triage with the policy snapshot.

youtube.description: Use ufw status verbose to read firewall defaults and inbound rules.

title: List Numbered UFW Rules
hook: Numbered rules make firewall review less ambiguous.
problem: You need a compact, ordered UFW rule list that can be discussed or reviewed without editing anything.
starting_state: A UFW fixture has numbered allow and deny rules.
what_changed: Nothing changes. UFW prints the ordered rule list.
when_to_use: Use when reviewing which inbound rules exist before considering a rule delete, insert, or policy change.
when_not_to_use: Do not delete rules by number from stale output; rule numbers can change after edits.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Numbered UFW rules with destination, action, and source.

shorts.title: Number firewall rules first.
shorts.voiceover: Before changing UFW, print numbered rules. It gives everyone the same ordered list to review.
shorts.caption: Review by number, edit later.

linkedin.question: Do you capture numbered firewall rules before changing UFW?
linkedin.cta: Use numbered output for review, not guesswork.

youtube.description: List ordered UFW rules safely with ufw status numbered.

title: Show the nftables Input Chain
hook: The packet path was hiding below UFW.
problem: You need to inspect the nftables input chain policy and the key accept or drop rules.
starting_state: A fixture has an nftables ruleset with an input chain and drop policy.
what_changed: Nothing changes. nftables prints rules and sed narrows the output to the input chain.
when_to_use: Use when UFW output is not enough or you need to verify the lower-level packet filter shape.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat a simplified chain excerpt as a complete network policy audit on hosts with multiple tables or namespaces.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: The nftables input chain with policy and selected TCP port rules.

shorts.title: Check the nftables input chain.
shorts.voiceover: When firewall behavior is confusing, inspect the nftables input chain and its default policy.
shorts.caption: Look below the friendly wrapper.

linkedin.question: When UFW output is not enough, do you inspect nftables directly?
linkedin.cta: Read the input chain before changing policy.

youtube.description: Inspect the nftables input chain and default policy.

title: Show iptables INPUT Rules
hook: Legacy firewall state can still explain live exposure.
problem: You need a concise view of the iptables INPUT chain policy and rules.
starting_state: A fixture has iptables-style INPUT rules for SSH, web traffic, mail, and database ports.
what_changed: Nothing changes. iptables prints the INPUT chain in a reusable rule format.
when_to_use: Use on systems where iptables rules may still be active or where tooling reports iptables syntax.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume iptables output covers nftables-native rules on every modern distribution.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: INPUT policy and append rules for accepted or dropped traffic.

shorts.title: Read iptables INPUT rules.
shorts.voiceover: On older or mixed systems, iptables output can explain why traffic is allowed or dropped.
shorts.caption: INPUT policy first.

linkedin.question: Do you still check iptables on mixed firewall hosts?
linkedin.cta: Capture the INPUT chain before editing rules.

youtube.description: Use iptables -S INPUT to inspect inbound firewall rules.

title: List Listening TCP Sockets
hook: Firewall rules matter after you know what is listening.
problem: You need to see which TCP sockets are listening and which process owns each one.
starting_state: A fixture has public web and SSH listeners plus local-only database services.
what_changed: Nothing changes. ss prints listening TCP sockets and process names where available.
when_to_use: Use before changing firewall rules, debugging exposure, or confirming a service actually bound a port.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat a listener as internet reachable without checking bind address and firewall policy together.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Listening TCP sockets with local address, port, peer wildcard, and process info.

shorts.title: List what is listening.
shorts.voiceover: Before firewall changes, list listening sockets. Bind address and process name tell you what could be exposed.
shorts.caption: Listeners first, firewall second.

linkedin.question: Do you list listening sockets before changing firewall rules?
linkedin.cta: Pair socket state with firewall policy.

youtube.description: Use ss -ltnp to list listening TCP services and processes.

title: Show Publicly Bound Listeners
hook: Localhost services are different from public listeners.
problem: You need to filter listening sockets down to services bound on all interfaces or IPv6 wildcard addresses.
starting_state: A fixture has a mix of public and localhost-only listeners.
what_changed: Nothing changes. awk filters socket output by bind address.
when_to_use: Use when you need a quick public-exposure worklist before comparing against firewall policy.
when_not_to_use: Do not ignore IPv6, container ports, or cloud firewalls on real hosts; this is the host-level socket view.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: The ss header plus listeners bound to public wildcard addresses.

shorts.title: Filter public listeners.
shorts.voiceover: A localhost database is not the same as a public service. Filter sockets by bind address first.
shorts.caption: Public bind addresses deserve review.

linkedin.question: Do you separate localhost listeners from public bind addresses during exposure checks?
linkedin.cta: Build the public listener worklist first.

youtube.description: Filter ss output to publicly bound listeners.

title: Find Allowed Ports with No Listener
hook: An open firewall rule can outlive the service it was created for.
problem: You need to find UFW-allowed ports that do not currently have a listening TCP socket.
command: comm -23 <(ufw status numbered | awk '/ALLOW/ {print}' | grep -Eo '[0-9]+/(tcp|udp)' | cut -d/ -f1 | sort -u) <(ss -ltnp | awk '/LISTEN/ {n=split($4,a,":"); print a[n]}' | sort -u)
starting_state: A fixture allows port 25 in UFW even though no service listens on port 25.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The shell compares allowed firewall ports with listening ports.
when_to_use: Use during firewall cleanup to find stale allow rules that no active service appears to need.
when_not_to_use: Do not remove rules automatically from this output; check UDP, scheduled services, maintenance windows, and external policy first.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Allowed port numbers that are absent from the current listening TCP socket list.

commands: comm -23 <(ufw status numbered | awk '/ALLOW/ {print}' | grep -Eo '[0-9]+/(tcp|udp)' | cut -d/ -f1 | sort -u) <(ss -ltnp | awk '/LISTEN/ {n=split($4,a,":"); print a[n]}' | sort -u)

shorts.title: Find stale allow rules.
shorts.voiceover: Compare UFW allow rules with listening sockets. If a port is allowed but nothing listens, it may be stale exposure.
shorts.caption: Allowed does not mean used.

linkedin.question: Do you look for firewall allow rules that no current listener uses?
linkedin.cta: Compare firewall policy with socket state.

youtube.description: Compare UFW allow rules with listening ports to find stale exposure.

title: Find Public Listeners Not Allowed by UFW
hook: The process was public, but the firewall did not mention it.
problem: You need to find publicly bound listening ports that are not listed as UFW allow rules.
command: comm -13 <(ufw status numbered | awk '/ALLOW/ {print}' | grep -Eo '[0-9]+/(tcp|udp)' | cut -d/ -f1 | sort -u) <(ss -ltnp | awk '$4 ~ /^(0[.]0[.]0[.]0|[[]::[]]|[*]):/ {n=split($4,a,":"); print a[n]}' | sort -u)
starting_state: A fixture has a public app listener on port 9000 that is not listed as an allowed UFW port.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The shell compares public listener ports with allowed firewall ports.
when_to_use: Use when checking for services that may be bound publicly without an explicit host firewall allow rule.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume the service is internet reachable without checking default policy, provider firewalls, container NAT, and IPv6.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Public listener port numbers that do not appear in UFW allow rules.

commands: comm -13 <(ufw status numbered | awk '/ALLOW/ {print}' | grep -Eo '[0-9]+/(tcp|udp)' | cut -d/ -f1 | sort -u) <(ss -ltnp | awk '$4 ~ /^(0[.]0[.]0[.]0|[[]::[]]|[*]):/ {n=split($4,a,":"); print a[n]}' | sort -u)

shorts.title: Find unlisted public listeners.
shorts.voiceover: Compare public listeners with allowed firewall ports. A wildcard-bound service outside policy deserves review.
shorts.caption: Public process, missing rule.

linkedin.question: Do you compare public listeners against firewall allow rules during hardening?
linkedin.cta: Find services outside the declared policy.

youtube.description: Compare publicly bound listeners with UFW allow rules.

title: Check Whether SSH Is Publicly Bound
hook: SSH can be locked down by source and still bind publicly.
problem: You need to see whether SSH is listening on a non-localhost address.
starting_state: A fixture has SSH listening on 0.0.0.0:22 with a source-restricted UFW rule.
what_changed: Nothing changes. awk filters socket output for non-local SSH bind addresses.
when_to_use: Use during SSH hardening checks or before deciding whether firewall source restrictions are enough.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat bind address alone as access policy; compare it with firewall sources and cloud security groups.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Any SSH listener on port 22 that is not bound only to localhost.

shorts.title: Is SSH publicly bound?
shorts.voiceover: Check whether SSH binds to a public address, then compare that with the firewall source rule.
shorts.caption: Bind address and source rule both matter.

linkedin.question: Do you check SSH bind address separately from firewall allow sources?
linkedin.cta: Review SSH exposure with both views.

youtube.description: Check whether SSH is listening on a public bind address.

title: Show Local-Only Database Listeners
hook: The database was listening, but only on localhost.
problem: You need to confirm common database ports are bound only to 127.0.0.1.
starting_state: A fixture has PostgreSQL and Redis listening on localhost ports.
what_changed: Nothing changes. awk filters socket output for local-only database listener ports.
when_to_use: Use during database exposure checks before changing bind addresses, firewalls, or application connection strings.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume every database uses only these ports; adapt the pattern for your stack.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Localhost-bound listeners on common database ports such as 5432 or 6379.

shorts.title: Confirm local-only databases.
shorts.voiceover: A database can listen safely when it is bound to localhost. Filter common database ports by local bind address.
shorts.caption: Local bind is part of exposure control.

linkedin.question: Do you verify database bind addresses before touching firewall rules?

youtube.description: Filter ss output for local-only database listeners.
